# Transcrição: 06-The Frame of Your Story

- **Fonte:** 06-The Frame of Your Story
- **Duração:** 07:25
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, the first thing we're going to do is to identify the frame of your story. Essentially where will the story exist, the beginning and the end? How will we choose those places? I actually think when it comes to storytelling, this is probably the most significant decision you're going to make. To begin, we have to understand something that is fundamental to stories. I have told you that stories are changes that take place over time, moments of transformation or realization. Here's another truth about those moments. They happen in an instant. I am convinced that almost every single moment of change that takes place in our lives happens in what I call a five second moment, which is to say the actual change, the moment you change your mind, the moment you realize you're a different person, the moment you look at the world in a new way, it happens almost instantaneously. It is always going to be a moment where you thought one thing one second before and now you think a new thing. We're looking for five second moments in our lives. In my spoon of power story, the five second moment for me, the moment of change was the moment Jamie put that spoon around my neck and I understood that this really is a magical thing that I now possess and will use in the future. It was the moment I became convinced that the spoon really had power, that the declaration I made months before as it emerged from a leaf pile, that declaration actually proved to be correct. I called it a spoon of power and now I believe it's a spoon of power. In my bad blood draw story, my five second moment, it's the moment the man touched his chin, showed me that white line and told me about the dog that once bit him. It was the moment I understood that he understood me. I'm trying to get to that five second moment. Stories are about five second moments of change and in order to create the frame of a story, we have to find those five second moments, those moments of change and they're always going to be the end of our story because they're the most important thing we're going to say. That five second moment at the end of the story is what we're aiming at. It is the goal that we have and when we find that moment, it makes storytelling so much easier because now we know what we have to do. Now we know the path we have to take and it makes all of our decisions so much easier. We're looking for that five second moment. It is the moment when Rose decides she's going to reject the wealth that she had before and live her own life. It's the moment Han Solo decides, I'm not going to go pay off job of the hut.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

I'm going to go save Luke Skywalker. It's the moment Harrison Ford says, close your eyes, Marion, because he believes in religion. These moments we're aiming at, the most important moments we have to speak. That is going to be the end of your story. To find the beginning of your story, to create the frame that we need for your story, we simply ask ourselves, what is the opposite of the end? What is the opposite of a man who believes in a spoon of power? It's a man who believes a spoon is a spoon. What is the opposite of a man understands that blood draws, needles don't have to be so bad after all. It is a man who believes that a blood draw and a needle are terrifying. That is how stories work. We start at the end because we know what we're aiming at and we need to know that. Now I'm a novelist. I write fiction as well. When I write fiction, it's entirely different. When I write fiction, I start at the beginning of a book with a premise, a question. I say things like, what if imaginary friends are real? Or what if there was a burglar who broke into people's homes but only stole things that would go unnoticed? And I write my book. I don't know what the end of my novel is because I haven't lived it yet. And because it's a novel, I get to make things up. I get to plot the course however I want. I can change things. I can add characters, take them out. When I'm writing a novel, it's about creation. But when I'm telling a true story from my life, it's not creation anymore. I have the facts already. I think of storytelling as puzzle building. I have an enormous pile of facts about a spoon of power. I have to choose which facts I want to include in the story and which facts I want to exclude. I have to decide what order to tell them in. I have to decide which points to emphasize. But the important part is I have to know what I'm aiming at. I have to know what I'm trying to say. Once I know what I'm trying to say, I can find the opposite of this and now I have the frame my story will exist in. Romantic comedies are the easiest example of this. At the beginning of just about every romantic comedy that has ever been made, two people don't love each other. And at the end of the movie, they love each other. That's how movies work. If you've ever seen the movie when Harry met Sally, it's the best example. Because at the beginning of that movie, Harry and Sally actually say they hate each other. And at the end of the movie, they love each other. It's why in the movie Bolt, I know how that movie's going to end by the first frame of the movie because I understand that when you're framing a movie, the beginning of the movie and the end of the movie are opposites of each other or approximately opposites of each

## [06:03 - 07:25]

other, at least in conversation with each other. They are somehow working against each other. And that's how we demonstrate change over time. And that's how we create a frame of a story. When we're creating that frame, I like to use a thesis statement. You've already heard me use them. It is, I used to be one person and then some stuff happened and now I'm a new person. I used to think one thing and now some stuff happened and now I think a new thing. Before I begin any story, I start with the thesis statement. I understand what I was and what I am now. And once I make those two decisions, once I create the frame of the story, it becomes so much easier to tell. Because everything I choose to put in my story must serve this purpose. Everything I put in my story must lead me to that moment, that thing I'm trying to say, the spoon of power is real. Blood draws don't have to be so bad if someone would just ask me why I'm so afraid. Once we know what we're trying to say, the beginning becomes evident and everything in the middle becomes a lot easier. So we start by creating a frame of our story, beginning and ending. That's what I'm going to have you do next.
